abstraction-with-base-interfaces.label: Abstract Base Classes and Interfaces
abstraction-with-base-interfaces.summary: Use `ABC` and `@abstractmethod` to enforce a shared method, then write helpers that depend on that behavior.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Make CatalogItem an abstract base class
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: In `models/catalog_item.py`, import `ABC` and `abstractmethod`, make `CatalogItem` inherit from `ABC`, and decorate `label(self)` with `@abstractmethod`. Keep the constructor that stores `title`. `main.py` checks that Python refuses to create an incomplete CatalogItem.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: Use `class CatalogItem(ABC):` and place `@abstractmethod` directly above `label`.

help.concept: Python has no `interface` keyword. An abstract base class is one way to declare and enforce a shared method contract.
help.hint_2: An abstract method can use `pass`; concrete child classes must implement it.

models_book_py.content: # Book is used in the next step; leave it unchanged for now.
# Book is used in the next step.

1.message: CatalogItem should be abstract so Python refuses to instantiate it.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Implement the abstract method in each child
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: `CatalogItem` is now abstract. Complete `Book.label()` and `Movie.label()` so both child classes become concrete. Each constructor can reuse the parent constructor with `super().__init__(title)`. Run `main.py` to print `Book: Dune` and `Movie: Moana`.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: A concrete child must implement every abstract method before Python can create it.

help.concept: `@abstractmethod` makes the requirement enforceable. A child that implements `label()` can be instantiated; an incomplete child cannot.
help.hint_2: Return the correct prefix from each `label()` implementation.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
for item in items:
    print(item.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
for item in items:
    print(item.label())

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
for item in items:
    print(item.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
for item in items:
    print(item.label())

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.title: Use a helper that trusts the interface
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.prompt: Complete `show_labels(items)` in `services/catalog_view.py`. Return one string with each item's `label()` on its own line. The helper does not need `isinstance` checks; it depends only on the shared behavior. `main.py` is already wired to print the returned string.
try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.hint: Collect each `item.label()` result and join them with `"\n"`.

help.concept: The ABC enforces the contract for this family of classes. The helper itself uses Python's duck-typing style: it only needs each object to provide `label()`.
help.hint_1: Use `"\n".join(...)` so there is no extra blank line.
help.hint_2: Do not print inside the helper; return one string.

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.starterCode:
# Run this provided script after completing show_labels.
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

main_py.content:
# Run this provided script after completing show_labels.
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

try_abstraction_with_base_interfaces_ske.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie
from services.catalog_view import show_labels

items = [Book("Dune"), Movie("Moana")]
print(show_labels(items))

services_catalog_view_py.content:
def show_labels(items):
    return "\n".join(item.label() for item in items)

0.message: Return both labels so main.py prints two lines.

sc-interface-purpose.title: Why use an abstract base class?
sc-interface-purpose.prompt: What does `ABC` with `@abstractmethod` add to this design?
sc-interface-purpose.hint: Think about what Python checks before an object can be created.

help.concept: An abstract base class turns the shared method expectation into an enforceable contract.
help.hint_1: Incomplete child classes cannot be instantiated.
help.hint_2: Concrete children still provide their own behavior.

options.a: Python prevents instantiation until the abstract method is implemented.
options.b: Every child must return identical text.
options.c: The parent class can no longer have a constructor.
options.d: The helper must check every exact subclass.

fb-interface-method-name.title: Add the abstract decorator
fb-interface-method-name.prompt: Fill in the decorator that marks a required method.

help.concept: `@abstractmethod` marks a method that concrete subclasses must implement.
help.hint_2: The word combines abstract and method.

dr-interface-build-order.title: Order the ABC workflow
dr-interface-build-order.prompt: Put these steps in a clear order.
dr-interface-build-order.hint: Declare the contract before creating concrete child objects.

help.concept: Import the ABC tools, define the abstract parent, implement the method in children, then use the objects through shared behavior.
help.hint_2: The helper comes after concrete implementations exist.

tokens.t2: Define the abstract method on the base class
tokens.t3: Implement the method in each concrete child
tokens.t4: Call the shared method from a helper

sc-interface-helper-focus.title: Choose what the helper should care about
sc-interface-helper-focus.prompt: When `show_labels(items)` loops through mixed objects, what should it focus on most?
sc-interface-helper-focus.hint: Pick the shared contract, not the exact subclass.

help.concept: Good interface-based code depends on shared behavior instead of subclass-specific branches.
help.hint_1: The helper should not care whether the object is a Book or Movie.
help.hint_2: It only needs the shared method to exist.

options.a: The order the subclasses were written in the file
options.b: The exact subclass name for every object
options.d: How many times the constructor was called

sketch-0.title: Python can enforce a shared method contract

sketch-1.title: Concrete children implement the abstract method

sketch-2.title: Helpers can depend on shared behavior
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
A helper can work with any object that provides the expected method.

```python
def collect_messages(channels):
    return [channel.message() for channel in channels]
```

The ABC enforces the contract for related classes. The helper then follows Python's duck-typing style: it calls `message()` without branching on exact class names.
